answer:
**The following works in the Falsafa corpus explicitly engage with key European Enlightenment political concepts—such as liberty (as developed by Locke, Montesquieu, and others), the social contract and general will (Rousseau), climate's influence on laws and society (Montesquieu), and freedom of thought (core to Voltaire, Fichte, and others). These engagements involve direct critique, quotation, or analysis of those thinkers' ideas in political theory contexts like rights, government, society, and individual freedoms. No other works in the corpus do so; ancient Indic smṛti texts, medieval Kawi literature, Old English poetry, 19th/20th-century Urdu works, and unrelated French theory volumes lack such explicit references or discussions.[^1][^2]**

### Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I* (1824)
This work systematically critiques Enlightenment views on liberty's compatibility with civilization, savagery, nomadism, and industry. It quotes and refutes Rousseau extensively on savage liberty, the Social Contract, and degeneration via progress; Montesquieu on climate, nomads, and slavery; and others on race, culture, and societal forms.[^3][^4][^5][^6]

### Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II* (1824)
Continues Vol. I's analysis, applying liberty principles to modern industries while implicitly building on Enlightenment debates (e.g., Voltaire cited on economic history).[^7]

### Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I* (1837)
Directly dissects Enlightenment systems: Chapter 6 critiques the social contract as the basis of civil/political laws (targeting Rousseau); Chapter 7 analyzes the "general will" (Rousseau); mentions Diderot on sciences/arts' effects.[^8][^9]

### Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II–IV* (1837)
Engages via historical/comparative analysis of laws, slavery, and society, quoting Voltaire on morals, equality, and climate (contra Montesquieu/others); critiques Rousseau/Diderot on progress and intolerance.[^10][^11]

### Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I & II* (1834)
Frames property rights as flowing from liberty (contra slave-based ancient law, echoing Locke/Montesquieu critiques); engages slavery's political effects as in Enlightenment debates.[^12]

### Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens* (1793)
An Enlightenment-era pamphlet demanding "freedom of thought" from European princes, directly embodying and advancing political concepts of intellectual liberty against absolutism (Voltaire/Fichte tradition).[^13]

[^1]: No matches in searches for "Locke", "Kant", "\"social contract\"", "\"natural rights\"", "\"separation of powers\"", "Diderot" (beyond passing Comte/Dunoyer mentions), or "Enlightenment" outside listed works/indic poetry contexts. *Corpus-wide search results*.

[^2]: *list_works* confirms 37 works; only these slugs show political theory engagement per metadata/searches.

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-b8546d).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 4](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/04-v-influence-de-la-culture-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-2894f7).

[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/02-i-influence-de-la-race-sur-la-liberte/translation/#p-ee3cb6).

[^6]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [ch. 3](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/03-ii-influence-des-circonstances-exterieures-sur-la-/translation/#p-1f97b0).

[^7]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [ch. 15](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/15-v-application-de-ces-moyens-de-liberte-aux-diverse/translation/#p-82578c).

[^8]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 6](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/06-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-civil/translation/#p-549b42).

[^9]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [ch. 7](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/07-du-systeme-dans-lequel-on-considere-les-lois-comme-l/translation/).

[^10]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL II*, [ch. 6](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-ii-20331d/06-des-difficultes-que-presente-la-question-de-l-infl/translation/#p-bd7546).

[^11]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [ch. 23](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/23-de-l-inegalite-des-fortunes-produite-par-l-esclava/translation/#p-2a47ee).

[^12]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 2](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-considere-considerée-comme-une-condition-de-l/translation/).

[^13]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [ch. 1](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/).
